Be aware that the price of the ticket and attitude of the object management is awful. Price is much more than for other wonderful sites like collosum, Piza tower, neushwenstein etc. You can pick only one of available circuits. In our (4th) circuit you additionally had to choose between seeing hall of mirrors and temple of kondor... The view of course is beautiful. But imo mountains are better. No information boards available. Toilets, bus service, luggage storage additionally paid

Es un parque en una ciudad, ¿por qué hay que pagar para entrar?, ¿no llega con los impuestos de los limeños? Hay que pagar 5 soles por persona, incluso un niño de 5 años Además dentro hay muchas atracciones y en todas hay que pagar, incluso para ir al baño cobran El colmo es que hay bastantes zonas cerradas: el paseo por las columnas, la parte más próxima a la fuente de colores, las cercanías de la casa como de cuento, vallas también cerca de las mariposas... El espectáculo de luces y agua es bonito pero del resto, me llevé una gran decepción

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Nasca es con " S" las culturas del antiguo Perú no conocieron la "Z". Las Líneas de Nasca son Patrimonio Cultural de la humanidad y es la obra visual más compleja que para admirarla solo es posible volando. Es un plano con geometría compleja, figuras estilizadas como señales y acertijos para interpretar y descubrir. Mucho se debe a la Dra. María Reiche por dedicar su vida entera a Las Líneas de NASCA.

English y español (second paragraph). Be careful with this tourist trap, you will need a reservation to go in, however, this is not well explained on the normal internet search. Here is the scheme, a lot of tourists, mostly English speaking come without reservation and there are a couple of ”tourists guides", taxi drivers in reality impersonating being part of the museum staff, at the entrance letting you know that you will not be able to enter without the reservation. In exchange, they can take to another archeological site for a significant amount of money (varies from 400 to 250 PENS). If you try to make it to the reception window the taxi driver cut you off and let them know that you don't have reservations with the typical wink wink, so they don't do much to help you. At the end, if there are space available or no shows, you might get access to the museum. So, my recommendation is, if you don't have a reservation, do not even bother to show up. Now, the museum inside is ok, you will learn about the pre-inka civilization in the area and their worship to the god of the sea. The your is mandatory, it last just one hour, and is quite entertaining, if you are into this kind of attractions. Cuidado con esta trampa para turistas. TIENE que tener reservación para entrar al museo. Si no tiene reservación vas a encontrar a un par de buitres con carnet de taxistas haciéndose pasar por guías turísticos y empleados del museo (que no son) para llevarte a otros sitios arqueológicos a 30 minutos en carro por una suma considerable suma (250 a 400 soles). Los taxistas se comunican con los empleados del museo para dejarlos saber que no tienes reserva con el típico guiño de ojo para que no traten de ayudarte mucho para clavarte con el tour del taxista. Al final, so hay cupo y presentas presión, en el museo te venden los tickets, pero eso es ya cosa de suerte. Asi, que si no tienes reservación, ni trates de llegar al museo, a menos que te quieras correr la suerte. Ahora, ya dentro del museo, el tour es mandatario, y esta muy entretenido. Sólo dura una hora.

We stayed at the Aranwa Resorts, and Spa, Paracas, Peru for two nights in May, 2018. The suite given to us was quite spacious and had a balcony overlooking the Paracas Bay. Even though the hotel is located on a sprawling area, there was no coffee maker or kettle in the room, the refrigerator was missing even though there was space in the modular unit in the suite. There was no running hot water in the room on the second day of our stay. We informed the front desk of the issue, but they did not rectify the problem satisfactory to us. Also no one speaks even simple English and communication with the hotel staff was a major issue. Based on the above we are giving a 3 star rating inspite of the resort having fantastic facilities.

Otro detalle es que esos carros con muchas luces que alquilan en el lugar, casi atropellan a un niño, super peligroso. Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

Infos/Tips: - Entrance is 45 SOL, but it’s worth it - There are audio guides through the iOS and Android applications "Monasterio de Santa Catalina" (Note that it supports Spanish, English and French, and will take the phone’s configured language) - It takes at least 2 hours to do the full tour of it (we took 2 hours and 15 minutes) - I would recommend to do it when it’s sunny/hot in the day, as it’s quite "cooler" in the museum - They accept VISA Founded in 1579 by Maria de Guzman, the Monastery of Santa Catalina is a sprawling complex that covers over 20,000 square meters. It served as a cloister for nuns of the Dominican Second Order and is renowned for its vibrant architecture and well-preserved colonial-era buildings. The monastery features a unique blend of Spanish colonial and native architectural styles, prominently using sillar, the white volcanic stone typical of Arequipa. The buildings are characterized by brightly colored walls in shades of blue, orange, and red, which create a striking visual impact and are complemented by beautiful arches, courtyards, and fountains. The monastery is organized into different sectors, including living quarters, chapels, plazas, streets, and gardens. Each sector served a specific purpose, reflecting the monastic life of prayer, work, and community.

Es un lugar impresionante, es muy recomendable ir con un guia turistico o con información impresa si realmente quieres conocer la historia del lugar y entender el por qué de su formación, descubrimiento e historia. Clima muy variable, llevar gorra, poncho, ropa ligera pero algo cubridor para el frio. Respeta en todo momento a los vigilantes y lugareños, y si puedes platica con ellos para q te orienten sobre las energias del lugar y las montañas aledañas.

Wait until the place opens up fully again, then it should be very nice.

Fui ayer domingo con mis hijos pequeños , con toda la ilusión de entrar como llegamos a las 5 :30 preguntamos por el recorrido nocturno nos informaron en una de las porterías que abrían nuevamente a las 7 de la noche y que el ingreso era por otra portería , permanecí cerca esperando la hora de apertura , me presente en la portería indicada y no había nadie , camine hacia la otra portería donde me indicaron que podría ser que el ingreso era por el restaurante y llego y efectivamente había ingreso pero solo al restaurante y el recorrido nocturno se había cancelado . Como puede ser que se cancele y los mismos empleados no se enteren para dar una información real al visitante? , en mi caso iba a pie con mi niña de brazos y mi hijo mayor y le di la vuelta completa al sitio buscando como ingresar ...para nada.

The tourist trap to end all tourist traps. If you have absolutely nothing else to do and 40 soles to spare...save it and just walk around some of the older cobblestoned neighborhoods for free, then take yourself out to a nice restaurant later. You're paying 40 soles to see red and blue paint.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Probably a nice museum - if you understand Spanish perfectly. Only in the beginning there were some English translations but then everything is only in Spanish. So without understanding anything there were some nice jewelry and pottery but also these were almost always the same. According to the guest book at the end there are almost only Perú citizens visiting this site. I guess more foreigners would come if they’d understand anything. Audio guides would be the perfect solution.

The Colca Canyon is a canyon of the Colca River in southern Peru, located about 160 kilometres northwest of Arequipa. With a depth of about 1000 - 2000 m, it is one of the deepest canyons in the world. Its length is about 70 kilometres. The Colca Valley is a colorful Andean valley with pre-Inca rooted inhabitants, and towns founded in Spanish colonial times, still inhabited by people of the Collagua and the Cabana cultures. The local people maintain their ancestral traditions and continue to cultivate the pre-Inca stepped terraces, called andenes. It is Peru's third most-visited tourist destination with about 120,000 visitors annually.
Easter light show was amazing! Great family atmosphere - so much fun seeing so many kids and adults out running through the water together!

1) YOU CANNOT WALK BACK, OR REPEAT ANY PART OF YOUR CIRCUIT. There are staff watching and they will blow their whistle and shout at you if you try. We arrived in a downpour so we walked to some shelter and waited it out. When we tried to walk back on the circuit we had paid for, we were not allowed. It doesn't matter that it's not busy, you want to take photos or any other reason, they will not let you walk back, even 20m. Unfortunately this was not clear when we purchased the ticket so we missed a significant portion of our route :( Therefore I'd advise you to walk slowly and enjoy the views, because once you arrive at the exit, that will be the end of your time at macchu pichu. I feel the staff lack compassion since it was 7am and quiet at macchu pichu, and I didn't want it to rain. If they had a cafe inside at the beginning I'd have waited there, but you have to enter at your time slot so I was left in a downpour in my poncho. I'd paid for flights, accomodation, and so many buses and trains to be there. The staff didn't show any compassion for my situation and were curt. <- therefore one star, but then I added one star for the staff that let me go take pictures of the llamas :) 2) TOILETS WILL COST YOU EXTRA ON TOP OF THE ENTRANCE FEE, AND BAGGAGE STORAGE. Which I also find ridiculous considering the entrance ticket is so expensive. Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

Beautiful and breathtaking place, with amazing colours and impression's. The price is more than barrable, and what you get is tons compared. There are several "areas" to discover and be carried away off the many piticually wonderful colours. In the far corner, they have a cozy little café, where they serve a minor selection off both cold and worm drinsk, cake and smaller dishes. It is perfectly located in arequipa, so due to the site itself and it's location, it is definitely a must see when going to this great south peruvean city. Before exploring the vast options of nature beauty places surrounding this city and its history.

Was a bit disappointed, circulating was complicated by gates and the presence of police, making walimg around a less pleasant experience. destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: One of a kind museum. Pay for the guided tour. It's less than $10usd and covers about 1.5hrs of history. They don't let you take cell phones into the museum. That's the only negative side. The jewelry is out of this world and you will be amazed! Definitely a museum worth visiting if you travel to Chiclayo/Lambayeque any time while in Peru!! First Sunday of all months the 10 soles entrance is free.

Como muchos otros comentan, ya se perdió la esencia de lo que era el “Circuito mágico del agua”, ahora parece una feria o parque de diversiones donde la atracción principal son esos juegos llenos de luces y ruido en lugar de las fuentes de agua que solían ser lo que todos íbamos a ver. Me da mucha pena como está todo ahora, porque ya casi ni hay áreas verdes a la vista, todo es kiosko, juegos mecánicos o inflables (y para colmo, a precios elevadísimos). Como cereza del pastel, ya ni te dejan ver el total de fuentes de agua si no pagas antes 5 soles extra para cruzar el túnel. Pésimo como arruinaron el parque de las aguas.

Les encantará.

Just prepare for the 3-5000 people that visit everyday.

Two stars compared to my expectations. It's a beautiful place, but overrun with people, expensive to get to, and v touristy. Think Disney meets Williamsburg. I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: It's a full day trip from Lima and the flight is really only 20 minutes of viewing the lines. The bus and the lines are actually not so bad, but what people forget to consider is that the planes are 6 seaters and are really shaky and induce dizziness so the experience itself is an exercise in trying to squint to see the lines while not vommitting. The town itself has a couple worthwhile eateries, but I wouldn't spend the night other than to break up the trip.

Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

Fui al circuito 1-C el 17 de abril a las 8:00 am y me tocó lluvia y niebla. No obstante despeja por momentos y se puede apreciar la vista desde la casa del guardian al que se tiene acceso, pero por la condición climática descarté la subida que ofrecía la ruta porque no valía la pena. Mejor reservar para entrar al medio día, vas a la fija. Aunque el paisaje es encantador por las montañas, incluso desde el tren, me pareció que tanto sacrificio y dinero para llegar hasta allí está un poco sobrevalorado. Demasiados turistas.

What an amazing experience! We were lucky enough to have gotten tickets to hike up Waynapicchu. It was a steep climb to get to the top but so worth it because it overlooks Machu Picchu (first picture) . It's better to go early in the morning to beat the crowd. We got on the bus from Aguas Calientes at 6 am. It takes about 30 minutes to reach Machu Picchu. We entered the gate a little after 7 am and was able ot enjoy everything with less people. Make sure you plan and get your tickets early as there is a daily limit.

Rating based only on the hike from Cabanaconde down to the oasis and back. The walk is doable in a day but made unpleasant by a deteriorating path and aggressive horsemen leading pack animals among it. Start early as there is no shelter between top and bottom. The oasis was clearly once beautiful, but is now marred by current and failed development attempts. Rubbish disposal appears to be a real issue. For the 70 soles entry fee I would hope better care can be taken of this area going forward.

The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

Si vas en movilidad particular, para descanso y visitar Kuelap pueden quedarse en Nuevo Tingo hay hoteles, hospedajes y restaurantes, las oficinas y embarque para las telecabinas esta a cinco cuadras o 500 metros aproximadamente del centro de la ciudad. Previo al ingreso a la ciudadela de Kuelap encuentran señoras de la zona que venden bebidas, alimentos que te servirán para el recorrido, en la ciudadelas Kuelap existen recipientes para la basura. Llevar ropa para protegerse de la lluvia que se presenta inesperadamente. Ahora a disfrutar Kuelap. Precios ticket para telecabinas 20 soles por persona, adultos niños, ticket para ingreso a ciudadelas Kuelap 20 adultos, 10 universitario, 2 escolares (soles).

We bought our tickets online a week prior to when we wanted to attend. I am not sure if it was busier because it was Easter weekend, but the line was - without exaggeration - at least 1km long. Towards the front, there appeared to be 2 lines, and as we were trying to figure out which line we should be in since we already had tickets, there was a near riot of people shoving and starting fist fights and surging to get in. We wanted to see the show that was around 7pm, and as it was 6:50 we realized there was no way we would get in on time, nor did we want to get crushed trying to see a bloody fountain show, so we left.

Es un parque en una ciudad, ¿por qué hay que pagar para entrar?, ¿no llega con los impuestos de los limeños? Hay que pagar 5 soles por persona, incluso un niño de 5 años Además dentro hay muchas atracciones y en todas hay que pagar, incluso para ir al baño cobran El colmo es que hay bastantes zonas cerradas: el paseo por las columnas, la parte más próxima a la fuente de colores, las cercanías de la casa como de cuento, vallas también cerca de las mariposas... El espectáculo de luces y agua es bonito pero del resto, me llevé una gran decepción

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Nasca es con " S" las culturas del antiguo Perú no conocieron la "Z". Las Líneas de Nasca son Patrimonio Cultural de la humanidad y es la obra visual más compleja que para admirarla solo es posible volando. Es un plano con geometría compleja, figuras estilizadas como señales y acertijos para interpretar y descubrir. Mucho se debe a la Dra. María Reiche por dedicar su vida entera a Las Líneas de NASCA.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán

What a beautiful square, the best square that I saw in South America so far. Very old (hundreds of years old) and very culturally rich. For the beauty of that place, the photos speak for themselves.

English y español (second paragraph). Be careful with this tourist trap, you will need a reservation to go in, however, this is not well explained on the normal internet search. Here is the scheme, a lot of tourists, mostly English speaking come without reservation and there are a couple of ”tourists guides", taxi drivers in reality impersonating being part of the museum staff, at the entrance letting you know that you will not be able to enter without the reservation. In exchange, they can take to another archeological site for a significant amount of money (varies from 400 to 250 PENS). If you try to make it to the reception window the taxi driver cut you off and let them know that you don't have reservations with the typical wink wink, so they don't do much to help you. At the end, if there are space available or no shows, you might get access to the museum. So, my recommendation is, if you don't have a reservation, do not even bother to show up. Now, the museum inside is ok, you will learn about the pre-inka civilization in the area and their worship to the god of the sea. The your is mandatory, it last just one hour, and is quite entertaining, if you are into this kind of attractions. Cuidado con esta trampa para turistas. TIENE que tener reservación para entrar al museo. Si no tiene reservación vas a encontrar a un par de buitres con carnet de taxistas haciéndose pasar por guías turísticos y empleados del museo (que no son) para llevarte a otros sitios arqueológicos a 30 minutos en carro por una suma considerable suma (250 a 400 soles). Los taxistas se comunican con los empleados del museo para dejarlos saber que no tienes reserva con el típico guiño de ojo para que no traten de ayudarte mucho para clavarte con el tour del taxista. Al final, so hay cupo y presentas presión, en el museo te venden los tickets, pero eso es ya cosa de suerte. Asi, que si no tienes reservación, ni trates de llegar al museo, a menos que te quieras correr la suerte. Ahora, ya dentro del museo, el tour es mandatario, y esta muy entretenido. Sólo dura una hora.

We stayed at the Aranwa Resorts, and Spa, Paracas, Peru for two nights in May, 2018. The suite given to us was quite spacious and had a balcony overlooking the Paracas Bay. Even though the hotel is located on a sprawling area, there was no coffee maker or kettle in the room, the refrigerator was missing even though there was space in the modular unit in the suite. There was no running hot water in the room on the second day of our stay. We informed the front desk of the issue, but they did not rectify the problem satisfactory to us. Also no one speaks even simple English and communication with the hotel staff was a major issue. Based on the above we are giving a 3 star rating inspite of the resort having fantastic facilities.

Las piletas y el shows estuvo muy agradable e interesante el problema es que hay demasiados atracciones con costo adicional y eso no es tan agradable ya que a cada rato aumenta la inversión de la visita al lugar. Una de las cosas que mas me disgusto fue que para entrar al túnel subterráneo, ahora cobran adicional cuando anteriormente el pase era libre a todas las instalaciones del parque. Otro detalle es que esos carros con muchas luces que alquilan en el lugar, casi atropellan a un niño, super peligroso. No deberian estar ahi, otra cosa es que hay demasiados juegos y le quitan protagonismo a la esencia del parque, ya que antes los niños iban a divertirse con el agua, ahora lo único que les llama la atencion es la cantidad de juegos que han puesto por todo lugar.

Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

Infos/Tips: - Entrance is 45 SOL, but it’s worth it - There are audio guides through the iOS and Android applications "Monasterio de Santa Catalina" (Note that it supports Spanish, English and French, and will take the phone’s configured language) - It takes at least 2 hours to do the full tour of it (we took 2 hours and 15 minutes) - I would recommend to do it when it’s sunny/hot in the day, as it’s quite "cooler" in the museum - They accept VISA Founded in 1579 by Maria de Guzman, the Monastery of Santa Catalina is a sprawling complex that covers over 20,000 square meters. It served as a cloister for nuns of the Dominican Second Order and is renowned for its vibrant architecture and well-preserved colonial-era buildings. The monastery features a unique blend of Spanish colonial and native architectural styles, prominently using sillar, the white volcanic stone typical of Arequipa. The buildings are characterized by brightly colored walls in shades of blue, orange, and red, which create a striking visual impact and are complemented by beautiful arches, courtyards, and fountains. The monastery is organized into different sectors, including living quarters, chapels, plazas, streets, and gardens. Each sector served a specific purpose, reflecting the monastic life of prayer, work, and community.

The monastery features a unique blend of Spanish colonial and native architectural styles, prominently using sillar, the white volcanic stone typical of Arequipa. The buildings are characterized by brightly colored walls in shades of blue, orange, and red, which create a striking visual impact and are complemented by beautiful arches, courtyards, and fountains. The monastery is organized into different sectors, including living quarters, chapels, plazas, streets, and gardens. Each sector served a specific purpose, reflecting the monastic life of prayer, work, and community.

Audio guides would be the perfect solution.

The Colca Canyon is a canyon of the Colca River in southern Peru, located about 160 kilometres northwest of Arequipa. With a depth of about 1000 - 2000 m, it is one of the deepest canyons in the world. Its length is about 70 kilometres. The Colca Valley is a colorful Andean valley with pre-Inca rooted inhabitants, and towns founded in Spanish colonial times, still inhabited by people of the Collagua and the Cabana cultures. The local people maintain their ancestral traditions and continue to cultivate the pre-Inca stepped terraces, called andenes. It is Peru's third most-visited tourist destination with about 120,000 visitors annually.

1) YOU CANNOT WALK BACK, OR REPEAT ANY PART OF YOUR CIRCUIT. There are staff watching and they will blow their whistle and shout at you if you try. We arrived in a downpour so we walked to some shelter and waited it out. When we tried to walk back on the circuit we had paid for, we were not allowed. It doesn't matter that it's not busy, you want to take photos or any other reason, they will not let you walk back, even 20m. Unfortunately this was not clear when we purchased the ticket so we missed a significant portion of our route :( Therefore I'd advise you to walk slowly and enjoy the views, because once you arrive at the exit, that will be the end of your time at macchu pichu. I feel the staff lack compassion since it was 7am and quiet at macchu pichu, and I didn't want it to rain. If they had a cafe inside at the beginning I'd have waited there, but you have to enter at your time slot so I was left in a downpour in my poncho. I'd paid for flights, accomodation, and so many buses and trains to be there. The staff didn't show any compassion for my situation and were curt. <- therefore one star, but then I added one star for the staff that let me go take pictures of the llamas :) 2) TOILETS WILL COST YOU EXTRA ON TOP OF THE ENTRANCE FEE, AND BAGGAGE STORAGE. Which I also find ridiculous considering the entrance ticket is so expensive. Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

Unfortunately this was not clear when we purchased the ticket so we missed a significant portion of our route :( Therefore I'd advise you to walk slowly and enjoy the views, because once you arrive at the exit, that will be the end of your time at macchu pichu. I feel the staff lack compassion since it was 7am and quiet at macchu pichu, and I didn't want it to rain. If they had a cafe inside at the beginning I'd have waited there, but you have to enter at your time slot so I was left in a downpour in my poncho. I'd paid for flights, accomodation, and so many buses and trains to be there. The staff didn't show any compassion for my situation and were curt. <- therefore one star, but then I added one star for the staff that let me go take pictures of the llamas :) 2) TOILETS WILL COST YOU EXTRA ON TOP OF THE ENTRANCE FEE, AND BAGGAGE STORAGE. Which I also find ridiculous considering the entrance ticket is so expensive. Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

Beautiful and breathtaking place, with amazing colours and impression's. The price is more than barrable, and what you get is tons compared. There are several "areas" to discover and be carried away off the many piticually wonderful colours. In the far corner, they have a cozy little café, where they serve a minor selection off both cold and worm drinsk, cake and smaller dishes. It is perfectly located in arequipa, so due to the site itself and it's location, it is definitely a must see when going to this great south peruvean city. Before exploring the vast options of nature beauty places surrounding this city and its history.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: One of a kind museum. Pay for the guided tour. It's less than $10usd and covers about 1.5hrs of history. They don't let you take cell phones into the museum. That's the only negative side. The jewelry is out of this world and you will be amazed! Definitely a museum worth visiting if you travel to Chiclayo/Lambayeque any time while in Peru!! First Sunday of all months the 10 soles entrance is free.

Como muchos otros comentan, ya se perdió la esencia de lo que era el “Circuito mágico del agua”, ahora parece una feria o parque de diversiones donde la atracción principal son esos juegos llenos de luces y ruido en lugar de las fuentes de agua que solían ser lo que todos íbamos a ver. Me da mucha pena como está todo ahora, porque ya casi ni hay áreas verdes a la vista, todo es kiosko, juegos mecánicos o inflables (y para colmo, a precios elevadísimos). Como cereza del pastel, ya ni te dejan ver el total de fuentes de agua si no pagas antes 5 soles extra para cruzar el túnel. Pésimo como arruinaron el parque de las aguas.

El lugar histórico bonito. Se aprecia la cultura del pasado. Es muy concurrido por los turistas, el sitio es seguro. Una de las cosas malas es que nunca hay donde estacionar ... Tienes que parquear a cuadras del lugar. Pero bueno igual no pasa nada. Y lo otro es que hay un trabajador de alli algo mal educado, que cree saberlo todo, un poco antipático... Pero con ponerlo en su sitio basta. Es lamentable que tengan personal no apto para atender a los turistas y público en general. . . De igual forma. Mi recomendación es que vayan a visitar el lugar. Les encantará.

Be prepared for a painfully long journey. Travelled Incarail. No stops for a coffee or cigarette along your transfers and don't expect much in terms of food. 1 bread roll for £400 journey. At least being up the top is very beautiful and guides are excellent and understanding to the exhausting journey. They Could definitely improve the getting there experience. Just prepare for the 3-5000 people that visit everyday.

Two stars compared to my expectations. It's a beautiful place, but overrun with people, expensive to get to, and v touristy. Think Disney meets Williamsburg. I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: It's a full day trip from Lima and the flight is really only 20 minutes of viewing the lines. The bus and the lines are actually not so bad, but what people forget to consider is that the planes are 6 seaters and are really shaky and induce dizziness so the experience itself is an exercise in trying to squint to see the lines while not vommitting. The town itself has a couple worthwhile eateries, but I wouldn't spend the night other than to break up the trip.

Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

Fui al circuito 1-C el 17 de abril a las 8:00 am y me tocó lluvia y niebla. No obstante despeja por momentos y se puede apreciar la vista desde la casa del guardian al que se tiene acceso, pero por la condición climática descarté la subida que ofrecía la ruta porque no valía la pena. Mejor reservar para entrar al medio día, vas a la fija. Aunque el paisaje es encantador por las montañas, incluso desde el tren, me pareció que tanto sacrificio y dinero para llegar hasta allí está un poco sobrevalorado. Demasiados turistas.

What an amazing experience! We were lucky enough to have gotten tickets to hike up Waynapicchu. It was a steep climb to get to the top but so worth it because it overlooks Machu Picchu (first picture) . It's better to go early in the morning to beat the crowd. We got on the bus from Aguas Calientes at 6 am. It takes about 30 minutes to reach Machu Picchu. We entered the gate a little after 7 am and was able ot enjoy everything with less people. Make sure you plan and get your tickets early as there is a daily limit.

Rating based only on the hike from Cabanaconde down to the oasis and back. The walk is doable in a day but made unpleasant by a deteriorating path and aggressive horsemen leading pack animals among it. Start early as there is no shelter between top and bottom. The oasis was clearly once beautiful, but is now marred by current and failed development attempts. Rubbish disposal appears to be a real issue. For the 70 soles entry fee I would hope better care can be taken of this area going forward.

text_clean: A bit disappointing. The panamericana (the highway) ruins quite a few spots and to see all the figures (the lines aren't really that interesting) you need to travel quite a bit. We used just the observation towers and the distance and the haze makes experiencing it quite hard. Obviously getting a small plane to show you the figures is the other alternative, which we didn't do. I was hoping the museum (Maria Reiche) would give us mire insight, but that is more about her life and less about the lines...

The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

Previo al ingreso a la ciudadela de Kuelap encuentran señoras de la zona que venden bebidas, alimentos que te servirán para el recorrido, en la ciudadelas Kuelap existen recipientes para la basura. Llevar ropa para protegerse de la lluvia que se presenta inesperadamente. Ahora a disfrutar Kuelap. Precios ticket para telecabinas 20 soles por persona, adultos niños, ticket para ingreso a ciudadelas Kuelap 20 adultos, 10 universitario, 2 escolares (soles).

We bought our tickets online a week prior to when we wanted to attend. I am not sure if it was busier because it was Easter weekend, but the line was - without exaggeration - at least 1km long. Towards the front, there appeared to be 2 lines, and as we were trying to figure out which line we should be in since we already had tickets, there was a near riot of people shoving and starting fist fights and surging to get in. We wanted to see the show that was around 7pm, and as it was 6:50 we realized there was no way we would get in on time, nor did we want to get crushed trying to see a bloody fountain show, so we left.

No information boards available. Toilets, bus service, luggage storage additionally paid

Es un parque en una ciudad, ¿por qué hay que pagar para entrar?, ¿no llega con los impuestos de los limeños? Hay que pagar 5 soles por persona, incluso un niño de 5 años Además dentro hay muchas atracciones y en todas hay que pagar, incluso para ir al baño cobran El colmo es que hay bastantes zonas cerradas: el paseo por las columnas, la parte más próxima a la fuente de colores, las cercanías de la casa como de cuento, vallas también cerca de las mariposas... El espectáculo de luces y agua es bonito pero del resto, me llevé una gran decepción

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Nasca es con " S" las culturas del antiguo Perú no conocieron la "Z". Las Líneas de Nasca son Patrimonio Cultural de la humanidad y es la obra visual más compleja que para admirarla solo es posible volando. Es un plano con geometría compleja, figuras estilizadas como señales y acertijos para interpretar y descubrir. Mucho se debe a la Dra. María Reiche por dedicar su vida entera a Las Líneas de NASCA.

English y español (second paragraph). Be careful with this tourist trap, you will need a reservation to go in, however, this is not well explained on the normal internet search. Here is the scheme, a lot of tourists, mostly English speaking come without reservation and there are a couple of ”tourists guides", taxi drivers in reality impersonating being part of the museum staff, at the entrance letting you know that you will not be able to enter without the reservation. In exchange, they can take to another archeological site for a significant amount of money (varies from 400 to 250 PENS). If you try to make it to the reception window the taxi driver cut you off and let them know that you don't have reservations with the typical wink wink, so they don't do much to help you. At the end, if there are space available or no shows, you might get access to the museum. So, my recommendation is, if you don't have a reservation, do not even bother to show up. Now, the museum inside is ok, you will learn about the pre-inka civilization in the area and their worship to the god of the sea. The your is mandatory, it last just one hour, and is quite entertaining, if you are into this kind of attractions. Cuidado con esta trampa para turistas. TIENE que tener reservación para entrar al museo. Si no tiene reservación vas a encontrar a un par de buitres con carnet de taxistas haciéndose pasar por guías turísticos y empleados del museo (que no son) para llevarte a otros sitios arqueológicos a 30 minutos en carro por una suma considerable suma (250 a 400 soles). Los taxistas se comunican con los empleados del museo para dejarlos saber que no tienes reserva con el típico guiño de ojo para que no traten de ayudarte mucho para clavarte con el tour del taxista. Al final, so hay cupo y presentas presión, en el museo te venden los tickets, pero eso es ya cosa de suerte. Asi, que si no tienes reservación, ni trates de llegar al museo, a menos que te quieras correr la suerte. Ahora, ya dentro del museo, el tour es mandatario, y esta muy entretenido. Sólo dura una hora.

If you try to make it to the reception window the taxi driver cut you off and let them know that you don't have reservations with the typical wink wink, so they don't do much to help you. At the end, if there are space available or no shows, you might get access to the museum. So, my recommendation is, if you don't have a reservation, do not even bother to show up. Now, the museum inside is ok, you will learn about the pre-inka civilization in the area and their worship to the god of the sea. The your is mandatory, it last just one hour, and is quite entertaining, if you are into this kind of attractions. Cuidado con esta trampa para turistas. TIENE que tener reservación para entrar al museo. Si no tiene reservación vas a encontrar a un par de buitres con carnet de taxistas haciéndose pasar por guías turísticos y empleados del museo (que no son) para llevarte a otros sitios arqueológicos a 30 minutos en carro por una suma considerable suma (250 a 400 soles). Los taxistas se comunican con los empleados del museo para dejarlos saber que no tienes reserva con el típico guiño de ojo para que no traten de ayudarte mucho para clavarte con el tour del taxista. Al final, so hay cupo y presentas presión, en el museo te venden los tickets, pero eso es ya cosa de suerte. Asi, que si no tienes reservación, ni trates de llegar al museo, a menos que te quieras correr la suerte. Ahora, ya dentro del museo, el tour es mandatario, y esta muy entretenido. Sólo dura una hora.

We stayed at the Aranwa Resorts, and Spa, Paracas, Peru for two nights in May, 2018. The suite given to us was quite spacious and had a balcony overlooking the Paracas Bay. Even though the hotel is located on a sprawling area, there was no coffee maker or kettle in the room, the refrigerator was missing even though there was space in the modular unit in the suite. There was no running hot water in the room on the second day of our stay. We informed the front desk of the issue, but they did not rectify the problem satisfactory to us. Also no one speaks even simple English and communication with the hotel staff was a major issue. Based on the above we are giving a 3 star rating inspite of the resort having fantastic facilities.

Las piletas y el shows estuvo muy agradable e interesante el problema es que hay demasiados atracciones con costo adicional y eso no es tan agradable ya que a cada rato aumenta la inversión de la visita al lugar. Una de las cosas que mas me disgusto fue que para entrar al túnel subterráneo, ahora cobran adicional cuando anteriormente el pase era libre a todas las instalaciones del parque. Otro detalle es que esos carros con muchas luces que alquilan en el lugar, casi atropellan a un niño, super peligroso. No deberian estar ahi, otra cosa es que hay demasiados juegos y le quitan protagonismo a la esencia del parque, ya que antes los niños iban a divertirse con el agua, ahora lo único que les llama la atencion es la cantidad de juegos que han puesto por todo lugar.

Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

Infos/Tips: - Entrance is 45 SOL, but it’s worth it - There are audio guides through the iOS and Android applications "Monasterio de Santa Catalina" (Note that it supports Spanish, English and French, and will take the phone’s configured language) - It takes at least 2 hours to do the full tour of it (we took 2 hours and 15 minutes) - I would recommend to do it when it’s sunny/hot in the day, as it’s quite "cooler" in the museum - They accept VISA Founded in 1579 by Maria de Guzman, the Monastery of Santa Catalina is a sprawling complex that covers over 20,000 square meters. It served as a cloister for nuns of the Dominican Second Order and is renowned for its vibrant architecture and well-preserved colonial-era buildings. The monastery features a unique blend of Spanish colonial and native architectural styles, prominently using sillar, the white volcanic stone typical of Arequipa. The buildings are characterized by brightly colored walls in shades of blue, orange, and red, which create a striking visual impact and are complemented by beautiful arches, courtyards, and fountains. The monastery is organized into different sectors, including living quarters, chapels, plazas, streets, and gardens. Each sector served a specific purpose, reflecting the monastic life of prayer, work, and community.

Es un lugar impresionante, es muy recomendable ir con un guia turistico o con información impresa si realmente quieres conocer la historia del lugar y entender el por qué de su formación, descubrimiento e historia. Clima muy variable, llevar gorra, poncho, ropa ligera pero algo cubridor para el frio. Respeta en todo momento a los vigilantes y lugareños, y si puedes platica con ellos para q te orienten sobre las energias del lugar y las montañas aledañas.

Areas like Miraflores and Barranco felt more secure than the city center.

The streets were blocked with trash and feces, making it difficult to navigate. To stay safe, we kept our distance and observed from afar. Areas like Miraflores and Barranco felt more secure than the city center.

Fui ayer domingo con mis hijos pequeños , con toda la ilusión de entrar como llegamos a las 5 :30 preguntamos por el recorrido nocturno nos informaron en una de las porterías que abrían nuevamente a las 7 de la noche y que el ingreso era por otra portería , permanecí cerca esperando la hora de apertura , me presente en la portería indicada y no había nadie , camine hacia la otra portería donde me indicaron que podría ser que el ingreso era por el restaurante y llego y efectivamente había ingreso pero solo al restaurante y el recorrido nocturno se había cancelado . Como puede ser que se cancele y los mismos empleados no se enteren para dar una información real al visitante? , en mi caso iba a pie con mi niña de brazos y mi hijo mayor y le di la vuelta completa al sitio buscando como ingresar ...para nada.

Una playa cristalina para compartir en familia, solo deben tomar en cuenta estos consejos: 1. No ir los fines de semana porque va mucha gente y no conseguiras espacio para acomodarte, ademas que el agua se vuelve reboltosa. 2. No llevar mascotas 3. No llevae bebidas alcoholicas ni frutas 4. No llevar envases de plasticos 5. No llevar cornetas de musica 6. No tienen duchas Es un lugar ideal para disfrutar entre semana

Una fiesta para el ojo, lleno de colores y texturas. Personalmente me parece un poco tenebroso y mal vibroso en general pues igual es historia religiosa es así. Para el que le gusta la fotografía será un festín, para el que no, no lo recomiendo porque tampoco la entrada es muy accesible. Dentro hay una cafetería con precios normales, el monasterio es enorme y puede tomar horas recorrerlo. Asegúrate de llevar zapatos cómodos, algo de tomar y snacks.

The tourist trap to end all tourist traps. If you have absolutely nothing else to do and 40 soles to spare...save it and just walk around some of the older cobblestoned neighborhoods for free, then take yourself out to a nice restaurant later. You're paying 40 soles to see red and blue paint.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Probably a nice museum - if you understand Spanish perfectly. Only in the beginning there were some English translations but then everything is only in Spanish. So without understanding anything there were some nice jewelry and pottery but also these were almost always the same. According to the guest book at the end there are almost only Perú citizens visiting this site. I guess more foreigners would come if they’d understand anything. Audio guides would be the perfect solution.

The Colca Canyon is a canyon of the Colca River in southern Peru, located about 160 kilometres northwest of Arequipa. With a depth of about 1000 - 2000 m, it is one of the deepest canyons in the world. Its length is about 70 kilometres. The Colca Valley is a colorful Andean valley with pre-Inca rooted inhabitants, and towns founded in Spanish colonial times, still inhabited by people of the Collagua and the Cabana cultures. The local people maintain their ancestral traditions and continue to cultivate the pre-Inca stepped terraces, called andenes. It is Peru's third most-visited tourist destination with about 120,000 visitors annually.
Easter light show was amazing! Great family atmosphere - so much fun seeing so many kids and adults out running through the water together!

1) YOU CANNOT WALK BACK, OR REPEAT ANY PART OF YOUR CIRCUIT. There are staff watching and they will blow their whistle and shout at you if you try. We arrived in a downpour so we walked to some shelter and waited it out. When we tried to walk back on the circuit we had paid for, we were not allowed. It doesn't matter that it's not busy, you want to take photos or any other reason, they will not let you walk back, even 20m. Unfortunately this was not clear when we purchased the ticket so we missed a significant portion of our route :( Therefore I'd advise you to walk slowly and enjoy the views, because once you arrive at the exit, that will be the end of your time at macchu pichu. I feel the staff lack compassion since it was 7am and quiet at macchu pichu, and I didn't want it to rain. If they had a cafe inside at the beginning I'd have waited there, but you have to enter at your time slot so I was left in a downpour in my poncho. I'd paid for flights, accomodation, and so many buses and trains to be there. The staff didn't show any compassion for my situation and were curt. <- therefore one star, but then I added one star for the staff that let me go take pictures of the llamas :) 2) TOILETS WILL COST YOU EXTRA ON TOP OF THE ENTRANCE FEE, AND BAGGAGE STORAGE. Which I also find ridiculous considering the entrance ticket is so expensive. Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

Beautiful and breathtaking place, with amazing colours and impression's. The price is more than barrable, and what you get is tons compared. It is perfectly located in arequipa, so due to the site itself and it's location, it is definitely a must see when going to this great south peruvean city. Before exploring the vast options of nature beauty places surrounding this city and its history.

text_clean: One of a kind museum. Pay for the guided tour. It's less than $10usd and covers about 1.5hrs of history. They don't let you take cell phones into the museum. First Sunday of all months the 10 soles entrance is free.

Como muchos otros comentan, ya se perdió la esencia de lo que era el “Circuito mágico del agua”, ahora parece una feria o parque de diversiones donde la atracción principal son esos juegos llenos de luces y ruido en lugar de las fuentes de agua que solían ser lo que todos íbamos a ver. Me da mucha pena como está todo ahora, porque ya casi ni hay áreas verdes a la vista, todo es kiosko, juegos mecánicos o inflables (y para colmo, a precios elevadísimos). Como cereza del pastel, ya ni te dejan ver el total de fuentes de agua si no pagas antes 5 soles extra para cruzar el túnel. Pésimo como arruinaron el parque de las aguas.

El lugar histórico bonito. Se aprecia la cultura del pasado. Es muy concurrido por los turistas, el sitio es seguro. Una de las cosas malas es que nunca hay donde estacionar ... Tienes que parquear a cuadras del lugar. Pero bueno igual no pasa nada. Y lo otro es que hay un trabajador de alli algo mal educado, que cree saberlo todo, un poco antipático... Pero con ponerlo en su sitio basta. Es lamentable que tengan personal no apto para atender a los turistas y público en general. . . De igual forma. Mi recomendación es que vayan a visitar el lugar. Les encantará.

Two stars compared to my expectations. It's a beautiful place, but overrun with people, expensive to get to, and v touristy. Think Disney meets Williamsburg. I did enjoy the hike up Machu Picchu mountain and the conversations with other travelers. We took the bus up, and hiked back down. The hike up is a serious climb, so be prepared! If you'd like to avoid the train, you can get a bus to a hydroelectric plant from Cusco and, from there, hike a couple hours to Aguas Calientes. Overall, the guides we heard didn't have knowledge that made it worth the cost. I'd recommend and audio tour online instead. There are more beautiful places and hikes to be found in the world.

text_clean: It's a full day trip from Lima and the flight is really only 20 minutes of viewing the lines. The bus and the lines are actually not so bad, but what people forget to consider is that the planes are 6 seaters and are really shaky and induce dizziness so the experience itself is an exercise in trying to squint to see the lines while not vommitting. The town itself has a couple worthwhile eateries, but I wouldn't spend the night other than to break up the trip.

Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

Fui al circuito 1-C el 17 de abril a las 8:00 am y me tocó lluvia y niebla. No obstante despeja por momentos y se puede apreciar la vista desde la casa del guardian al que se tiene acceso, pero por la condición climática descarté la subida que ofrecía la ruta porque no valía la pena. Mejor reservar para entrar al medio día, vas a la fija. Aunque el paisaje es encantador por las montañas, incluso desde el tren, me pareció que tanto sacrificio y dinero para llegar hasta allí está un poco sobrevalorado. Demasiados turistas.

What an amazing experience! We were lucky enough to have gotten tickets to hike up Waynapicchu. It was a steep climb to get to the top but so worth it because it overlooks Machu Picchu (first picture) . It's better to go early in the morning to beat the crowd. We got on the bus from Aguas Calientes at 6 am. It takes about 30 minutes to reach Machu Picchu. We entered the gate a little after 7 am and was able ot enjoy everything with less people. Make sure you plan and get your tickets early as there is a daily limit.

Rating based only on the hike from Cabanaconde down to the oasis and back. The walk is doable in a day but made unpleasant by a deteriorating path and aggressive horsemen leading pack animals among it. Start early as there is no shelter between top and bottom. The oasis was clearly once beautiful, but is now marred by current and failed development attempts. Rubbish disposal appears to be a real issue. For the 70 soles entry fee I would hope better care can be taken of this area going forward.

The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

Si vas en movilidad particular, para descanso y visitar Kuelap pueden quedarse en Nuevo Tingo hay hoteles, hospedajes y restaurantes, las oficinas y embarque para las telecabinas esta a cinco cuadras o 500 metros aproximadamente del centro de la ciudad. Previo al ingreso a la ciudadela de Kuelap encuentran señoras de la zona que venden bebidas, alimentos que te servirán para el recorrido, en la ciudadelas Kuelap existen recipientes para la basura. Llevar ropa para protegerse de la lluvia que se presenta inesperadamente. Ahora a disfrutar Kuelap. Precios ticket para telecabinas 20 soles por persona, adultos niños, ticket para ingreso a ciudadelas Kuelap 20 adultos, 10 universitario, 2 escolares (soles).

We bought our tickets online a week prior to when we wanted to attend. I am not sure if it was busier because it was Easter weekend, but the line was - without exaggeration - at least 1km long. Towards the front, there appeared to be 2 lines, and as we were trying to figure out which line we should be in since we already had tickets, there was a near riot of people shoving and starting fist fights and surging to get in. We wanted to see the show that was around 7pm, and as it was 6:50 we realized there was no way we would get in on time, nor did we want to get crushed trying to see a bloody fountain show, so we left.

Es un parque en una ciudad, ¿por qué hay que pagar para entrar?, ¿no llega con los impuestos de los limeños? Hay que pagar 5 soles por persona, incluso un niño de 5 años Además dentro hay muchas atracciones y en todas hay que pagar, incluso para ir al baño cobran El colmo es que hay bastantes zonas cerradas: el paseo por las columnas, la parte más próxima a la fuente de colores, las cercanías de la casa como de cuento, vallas también cerca de las mariposas... El espectáculo de luces y agua es bonito pero del resto, me llevé una gran decepción

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Nasca es con " S" las culturas del antiguo Perú no conocieron la "Z". Las Líneas de Nasca son Patrimonio Cultural de la humanidad y es la obra visual más compleja que para admirarla solo es posible volando. Es un plano con geometría compleja, figuras estilizadas como señales y acertijos para interpretar y descubrir. Mucho se debe a la Dra. María Reiche por dedicar su vida entera a Las Líneas de NASCA.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Shows the ancient burial of a very famous Peruvian Lord, The Lord of Sipan!
What a beautiful square, the best square that I saw in South America so far. Very old (hundreds of years old) and very culturally rich. For the beauty of that place, the photos speak for themselves.

Puedes sentirte tranquila y relajada Disfrutar de una buena arquitectura Tiene una catedral hermosa y Esta rodeada de bellas tiendas Donde encuentras de todo y para todos los gustos.Personalmente me encanta.

Yo me arriesgue rapidamente quitarme la mascarilla y pude tomar unas foticos que me encantaron.

English y español (second paragraph). Be careful with this tourist trap, you will need a reservation to go in, however, this is not well explained on the normal internet search. Here is the scheme, a lot of tourists, mostly English speaking come without reservation and there are a couple of ”tourists guides", taxi drivers in reality impersonating being part of the museum staff, at the entrance letting you know that you will not be able to enter without the reservation. In exchange, they can take to another archeological site for a significant amount of money (varies from 400 to 250 PENS). If you try to make it to the reception window the taxi driver cut you off and let them know that you don't have reservations with the typical wink wink, so they don't do much to help you. At the end, if there are space available or no shows, you might get access to the museum. So, my recommendation is, if you don't have a reservation, do not even bother to show up. Now, the museum inside is ok, you will learn about the pre-inka civilization in the area and their worship to the god of the sea. The your is mandatory, it last just one hour, and is quite entertaining, if you are into this kind of attractions. Cuidado con esta trampa para turistas. TIENE que tener reservación para entrar al museo. Si no tiene reservación vas a encontrar a un par de buitres con carnet de taxistas haciéndose pasar por guías turísticos y empleados del museo (que no son) para llevarte a otros sitios arqueológicos a 30 minutos en carro por una suma considerable suma (250 a 400 soles). Los taxistas se comunican con los empleados del museo para dejarlos saber que no tienes reserva con el típico guiño de ojo para que no traten de ayudarte mucho para clavarte con el tour del taxista. Al final, so hay cupo y presentas presión, en el museo te venden los tickets, pero eso es ya cosa de suerte. Asi, que si no tienes reservación, ni trates de llegar al museo, a menos que te quieras correr la suerte. Ahora, ya dentro del museo, el tour es mandatario, y esta muy entretenido. Sólo dura una hora.

We stayed at the Aranwa Resorts, and Spa, Paracas, Peru for two nights in May, 2018. The suite given to us was quite spacious and had a balcony overlooking the Paracas Bay. Even though the hotel is located on a sprawling area, there was no coffee maker or kettle in the room, the refrigerator was missing even though there was space in the modular unit in the suite. There was no running hot water in the room on the second day of our stay. We informed the front desk of the issue, but they did not rectify the problem satisfactory to us. Also no one speaks even simple English and communication with the hotel staff was a major issue. Based on the above we are giving a 3 star rating inspite of the resort having fantastic facilities.

Lugar increible, en mi opinión, la mejor playa del Perú, si lo q buscas es limpieza, tranquilidad (no tiene olas) y belleza natural. Ya tiene los servicios básicos como lugar de estacionamiento y servicios higiénicos. Al estar dentro de la Reserva Natural de Paracas, los negocios tipo restaurantes, están lejos.

Las piletas y el shows estuvo muy agradable e interesante el problema es que hay demasiados atracciones con costo adicional y eso no es tan agradable ya que a cada rato aumenta la inversión de la visita al lugar. Una de las cosas que mas me disgusto fue que para entrar al túnel subterráneo, ahora cobran adicional cuando anteriormente el pase era libre a todas las instalaciones del parque. Otro detalle es que esos carros con muchas luces que alquilan en el lugar, casi atropellan a un niño, super peligroso. No deberian estar ahi, otra cosa es que hay demasiados juegos y le quitan protagonismo a la esencia del parque, ya que antes los niños iban a divertirse con el agua, ahora lo único que les llama la atencion es la cantidad de juegos que han puesto por todo lugar.

Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

Infos/Tips: - Entrance is 45 SOL, but it’s worth it - There are audio guides through the iOS and Android applications "Monasterio de Santa Catalina" (Note that it supports Spanish, English and French, and will take the phone’s configured language) - It takes at least 2 hours to do the full tour of it (we took 2 hours and 15 minutes) - I would recommend to do it when it’s sunny/hot in the day, as it’s quite "cooler" in the museum - They accept VISA Founded in 1579 by Maria de Guzman, the Monastery of Santa Catalina is a sprawling complex that covers over 20,000 square meters. It served as a cloister for nuns of the Dominican Second Order and is renowned for its vibrant architecture and well-preserved colonial-era buildings. The monastery features a unique blend of Spanish colonial and native architectural styles, prominently using sillar, the white volcanic stone typical of Arequipa. The buildings are characterized by brightly colored walls in shades of blue, orange, and red, which create a striking visual impact and are complemented by beautiful arches, courtyards, and fountains. The monastery is organized into different sectors, including living quarters, chapels, plazas, streets, and gardens. Each sector served a specific purpose, reflecting the monastic life of prayer, work, and community.

Respeta en todo momento a los vigilantes y lugareños, y si puedes platica con ellos para q te orienten sobre las energias del lugar y las montañas aledañas.

Fui ayer domingo con mis hijos pequeños , con toda la ilusión de entrar como llegamos a las 5 :30 preguntamos por el recorrido nocturno nos informaron en una de las porterías que abrían nuevamente a las 7 de la noche y que el ingreso era por otra portería , permanecí cerca esperando la hora de apertura , me presente en la portería indicada y no había nadie , camine hacia la otra portería donde me indicaron que podría ser que el ingreso era por el restaurante y llego y efectivamente había ingreso pero solo al restaurante y el recorrido nocturno se había cancelado . Como puede ser que se cancele y los mismos empleados no se enteren para dar una información real al visitante? , en mi caso iba a pie con mi niña de brazos y mi hijo mayor y le di la vuelta completa al sitio buscando como ingresar ...para nada.

Una playa cristalina para compartir en familia, solo deben tomar en cuenta estos consejos: 1. No ir los fines de semana porque va mucha gente y no conseguiras espacio para acomodarte, ademas que el agua se vuelve reboltosa. 2. No llevar mascotas 3. No llevae bebidas alcoholicas ni frutas 4. No llevar envases de plasticos 5. No llevar cornetas de musica 6. No tienen duchas Es un lugar ideal para disfrutar entre semana

So without understanding anything there were some nice jewelry and pottery but also these were almost always the same. According to the guest book at the end there are almost only Perú citizens visiting this site. I guess more foreigners would come if they’d understand anything. Audio guides would be the perfect solution.
